exercises.name: 100 Pullup Program
exercises.simplyPut: Build strength in your full body with this advanced-level exercise. 100 Pullup Program targets the full body while also engaging supporting muscle groups for balanced development.

mistakes.error: Ego lifting — using too much weight with poor form
mistakes.consequence: Disc herniation, muscle tears, joint damage
mistakes.correction: Reduce weight 20-30%; master form before adding load; video yourself

mistakes.consequence: Cold tissue tears, reduced performance
mistakes.correction: 2-3 progressive warm-up sets: empty bar → 50% → 70% → working weight

mistakes.consequence: Lumbar disc herniation (L4-L5, L5-S1 most common)
mistakes.correction: Hip hinge drill; brace before lifting; if you can't maintain neutral spine, weight is too heavy

mistakes.consequence: Reduced muscle activation; tendon overload at bottom position
mistakes.correction: 2-second pause at bottom of each rep; control the eccentric

mistakes.consequence: Muscle imbalances leading to compensatory injury over time
mistakes.correction: Include single-arm/single-leg work 1-2x per week

mistakes.consequence: Acute injury becomes chronic; compensation patterns develop
mistakes.correction: Pain ≠ effort. Sharp, shooting, or joint pain = stop. Muscle burn during set = normal

physiologyLinks.effect: Sessions >75 min elevate cortisol, entering catabolic state
GLUT4 translocation increases glucose uptake in trained muscle by 40%
Compound lifts increase testosterone 15-30% for 30 min post-exercise (Kraemer 1999)
Mechanical loading stimulates osteoblast activity via Wolff's law
High-volume training (10+ sets) elevates GH up to 400% (Wideman 2002)
Resistance exercise elevates MPS for 24-48 hours post-session

exercises.name: Barbell Hip Thrust
exercises.simplyPut: Sit with your upper back on a bench, roll a heavy barbell over your hips, and thrust your hips upward. This is the single best exercise for building strong glutes — the largest muscles in your body. Strong glutes power every athletic movement from sprinting to jumping.
exercises.howTo:
1. Sit on floor with upper back against bench, bar over hips
2. Use a barbell pad for comfort
3. Feet flat on floor, about hip-width, 12-16 inches from glutes
4. Drive through heels, squeezing glutes to lift hips
5. At top: shoulders on bench, hips fully extended, shins vertical
6. Hold top position for 1-2 seconds, squeeze glutes hard
7. Lower with control
8. Don't hyperextend lower back at top

mistakes.error: Ego lifting — using too much weight with poor form
mistakes.consequence: Disc herniation, muscle tears, joint damage
mistakes.correction: Reduce weight 20-30%; master form before adding load; video yourself

mistakes.consequence: Cold tissue tears, reduced performance
mistakes.correction: 2-3 progressive warm-up sets: empty bar → 50% → 70% → working weight

mistakes.consequence: Lumbar disc herniation (L4-L5, L5-S1 most common)
mistakes.correction: Hip hinge drill; brace before lifting; if you can't maintain neutral spine, weight is too heavy

mistakes.consequence: Reduced muscle activation; tendon overload at bottom position
mistakes.correction: 2-second pause at bottom of each rep; control the eccentric

mistakes.consequence: Muscle imbalances leading to compensatory injury over time
mistakes.correction: Include single-arm/single-leg work 1-2x per week

mistakes.consequence: Acute injury becomes chronic; compensation patterns develop
mistakes.correction: Pain ≠ effort. Sharp, shooting, or joint pain = stop. Muscle burn during set = normal

physiologyLinks.effect: Mechanical loading stimulates osteoblast activity via Wolff's law
Acute increase during lifting, chronic reduction of 3-5 mmHg systolic
High-volume training (10+ sets) elevates GH up to 400% (Wideman 2002)
GLUT4 translocation increases glucose uptake in trained muscle by 40%

mistakes.correction: Reduce weight 20-30%; master form before adding load; video yourself

mistakes.consequence: Cold tissue tears, reduced performance
mistakes.correction: 2-3 progressive warm-up sets: empty bar → 50% → 70% → working weight

mistakes.consequence: Lumbar disc herniation (L4-L5, L5-S1 most common)
mistakes.correction: Hip hinge drill; brace before lifting; if you can't maintain neutral spine, weight is too heavy

mistakes.consequence: Reduced muscle activation; tendon overload at bottom position
mistakes.correction: 2-second pause at bottom of each rep; control the eccentric

mistakes.consequence: Muscle imbalances leading to compensatory injury over time
mistakes.correction: Include single-arm/single-leg work 1-2x per week

mistakes.consequence: Acute injury becomes chronic; compensation patterns develop
mistakes.correction: Pain ≠ effort. Sharp, shooting, or joint pain = stop. Muscle burn during set = normal

physiologyLinks.effect: Sessions >75 min elevate cortisol, entering catabolic state
GLUT4 translocation increases glucose uptake in trained muscle by 40%
Compound lifts increase testosterone 15-30% for 30 min post-exercise (Kraemer 1999)
High-volume training (10+ sets) elevates GH up to 400% (Wideman 2002)
Resistance exercise elevates MPS for 24-48 hours post-session
Mechanical loading stimulates osteoblast activity via Wolff's law

mistakes.consequence: Disc herniation, muscle tears, joint damage
mistakes.correction: Reduce weight 20-30%; master form before adding load; video yourself

mistakes.consequence: Cold tissue tears, reduced performance
mistakes.correction: 2-3 progressive warm-up sets: empty bar → 50% → 70% → working weight

mistakes.consequence: Lumbar disc herniation (L4-L5, L5-S1 most common)
mistakes.correction: Hip hinge drill; brace before lifting; if you can't maintain neutral spine, weight is too heavy

mistakes.consequence: Reduced muscle activation; tendon overload at bottom position
mistakes.correction: 2-second pause at bottom of each rep; control the eccentric

mistakes.consequence: Muscle imbalances leading to compensatory injury over time
mistakes.correction: Include single-arm/single-leg work 1-2x per week

mistakes.consequence: Acute injury becomes chronic; compensation patterns develop
mistakes.correction: Pain ≠ effort. Sharp, shooting, or joint pain = stop. Muscle burn during set = normal

physiologyLinks.effect: Compound lifts increase testosterone 15-30% for 30 min post-exercise (Kraemer 1999)
Mechanical loading stimulates osteoblast activity via Wolff's law
High-volume training (10+ sets) elevates GH up to 400% (Wideman 2002)
Sessions >75 min elevate cortisol, entering catabolic state
Resistance exercise elevates MPS for 24-48 hours post-session

mistakes.correction: Reduce weight 20-30%; master form before adding load; video yourself

mistakes.consequence: Cold tissue tears, reduced performance
mistakes.correction: 2-3 progressive warm-up sets: empty bar → 50% → 70% → working weight

mistakes.consequence: Lumbar disc herniation (L4-L5, L5-S1 most common)
mistakes.correction: Hip hinge drill; brace before lifting; if you can't maintain neutral spine, weight is too heavy

mistakes.consequence: Reduced muscle activation; tendon overload at bottom position
mistakes.correction: 2-second pause at bottom of each rep; control the eccentric

mistakes.consequence: Muscle imbalances leading to compensatory injury over time
mistakes.correction: Include single-arm/single-leg work 1-2x per week

mistakes.consequence: Acute injury becomes chronic; compensation patterns develop
mistakes.correction: Pain ≠ effort. Sharp, shooting, or joint pain = stop. Muscle burn during set = normal

physiologyLinks.effect: High-volume training (10+ sets) elevates GH up to 400% (Wideman 2002)
GLUT4 translocation increases glucose uptake in trained muscle by 40%
Compound lifts increase testosterone 15-30% for 30 min post-exercise (Kraemer 1999)
Sessions >75 min elevate cortisol, entering catabolic state
Mechanical loading stimulates osteoblast activity via Wolff's law
Resistance exercise elevates MPS for 24-48 hours post-session

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
exercises.simplyPut: Hold dumbbells at your sides and curl them up to your shoulders by bending your elbows. The simplest arm exercise — it builds the muscles on the front of your upper arms that you flex in the mirror.
exercises.howTo:
1. Stand with dumbbells at sides, palms facing forward
2. Keep elbows pinned at your sides
3. Curl weights up by bending elbows only
4. Squeeze biceps hard at the top
5. Lower with control — don't just drop them
6. Don't swing body or use momentum
7. Full range of motion: straight arms at bottom, full contraction at top

mistakes.error: Ego lifting — using too much weight with poor form
mistakes.consequence: Disc herniation, muscle tears, joint damage
mistakes.correction: Reduce weight 20-30%; master form before adding load; video yourself

mistakes.consequence: Cold tissue tears, reduced performance
mistakes.correction: 2-3 progressive warm-up sets: empty bar → 50% → 70% → working weight

mistakes.consequence: Lumbar disc herniation (L4-L5, L5-S1 most common)
mistakes.correction: Hip hinge drill; brace before lifting; if you can't maintain neutral spine, weight is too heavy

mistakes.consequence: Reduced muscle activation; tendon overload at bottom position
mistakes.correction: 2-second pause at bottom of each rep; control the eccentric

mistakes.consequence: Muscle imbalances leading to compensatory injury over time
mistakes.correction: Include single-arm/single-leg work 1-2x per week

mistakes.consequence: Acute injury becomes chronic; compensation patterns develop
mistakes.correction: Pain ≠ effort. Sharp, shooting, or joint pain = stop. Muscle burn during set = normal

physiologyLinks.effect: GLUT4 translocation increases glucose uptake in trained muscle by 40%
Mechanical loading stimulates osteoblast activity via Wolff's law
High-volume training (10+ sets) elevates GH up to 400% (Wideman 2002)
Sessions >75 min elevate cortisol, entering catabolic state

exercises.name: Forearm Plank
exercises.simplyPut: Hold your body in a straight line, resting on your forearms and toes. It's like being a bridge — your core muscles are the cables holding everything up. This exercise strengthens your entire midsection without any movement.
exercises.howTo:
1. Lie face down, then prop yourself up on forearms (elbows under shoulders)
2. Extend legs behind, toes on floor
3. Lift hips to create a straight line from head to heels
4. Squeeze glutes and brace core as if bracing for a punch
5. Don't let hips sag down or pike up
6. Breathe normally — don't hold your breath
7. Look at the floor between your fists
8. Hold for time (start with 20 seconds, build to 60+)

exercises.name: Parallel Bar Dips
exercises.simplyPut: Support yourself between two parallel bars and lower your body by bending your arms, then push back up. Dips are the upper body squat — they work your chest, shoulders, and triceps harder than almost any other exercise.
exercises.howTo:
1. Grip parallel bars, arms straight, body supported
2. Lean slightly forward (more forward = more chest, upright = more triceps)
3. Lower body by bending elbows until upper arms are parallel to floor
4. Keep elbows close to body (not flared out)
5. Press back up to full lockout
6. Don't swing or use momentum
7. Control the descent — 2-3 seconds down

exercises.name: Pull-Up (Bodyweight)
exercises.simplyPut: Grab an overhead bar and pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar. It's you versus gravity — no machines, no help. Pull-ups build a powerful back, strong arms, and iron grip. Marines test them, climbers depend on them, and Navy SEALs do hundreds.
exercises.howTo:
1. Grip bar overhand (palms facing away), hands shoulder-width or slightly wider
2. Hang with arms fully extended (dead hang)
3. Engage lats by pulling shoulder blades down and back
4. Pull elbows down toward hips (think 'put elbows in back pockets')
5. Continue pulling until chin clears the bar
6. Lower with control — full extension at bottom
7. Don't kip, swing, or use momentum
8. Breathe out as you pull up, in as you lower

exercises.contraindications: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear, lateral epicondylitis. Start with band-assisted or negative pull-ups if you can't do one full rep.

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift
exercises.simplyPut: Hold a barbell and bend forward at the hips while keeping your legs nearly straight, feeling a deep stretch in your hamstrings, then stand back up. This strengthens the entire back of your body — hamstrings, glutes, and lower back.
exercises.howTo:
1. Stand holding barbell at hip height, arms straight
2. Feet hip-width, slight bend in knees (keep this bend constant)
3. Push hips BACK (not down) — like closing a car door with your butt
4. Lower bar along thighs and shins
5. Go until you feel a strong hamstring stretch (usually mid-shin level)
6. Keep bar close to body the entire time
7. Drive hips forward to stand, squeeze glutes at top
8. Don't round your back — maintain flat/neutral spine

exercises.name: Russian Kettlebell Swing
exercises.simplyPut: Swing a kettlebell between your legs and up to chest height using a powerful hip snap. This is the king of kettlebell exercises — it combines strength and cardio in one explosive movement. It builds an iron posterior chain (glutes, hamstrings, back) and bulletproof grip.
exercises.howTo:
1. Stand behind kettlebell, feet shoulder-width
2. Hinge at hips, grip bell with both hands
3. Hike the bell back between your legs (like a football snap)
4. Explosively snap hips forward — stand tall and squeeze glutes
5. Arms are just hooks — they don't pull the bell
6. Bell should float to chest height at the top
7. Let bell swing back between legs, absorb with hip hinge
8. Repeat with rhythmic hip snap — it's a hip exercise, not an arm exercise

exercises.name: Shoveling Simulation

mistakes.error: Ego lifting — using too much weight with poor form
mistakes.consequence: Disc herniation, muscle tears, joint damage
mistakes.correction: Reduce weight 20-30%; master form before adding load; video yourself

mistakes.consequence: Cold tissue tears, reduced performance
mistakes.correction: 2-3 progressive warm-up sets: empty bar → 50% → 70% → working weight

mistakes.consequence: Lumbar disc herniation (L4-L5, L5-S1 most common)
mistakes.correction: Hip hinge drill; brace before lifting; if you can't maintain neutral spine, weight is too heavy

mistakes.consequence: Reduced muscle activation; tendon overload at bottom position
mistakes.correction: 2-second pause at bottom of each rep; control the eccentric

mistakes.consequence: Muscle imbalances leading to compensatory injury over time
mistakes.correction: Include single-arm/single-leg work 1-2x per week

mistakes.consequence: Acute injury becomes chronic; compensation patterns develop
mistakes.correction: Pain ≠ effort. Sharp, shooting, or joint pain = stop. Muscle burn during set = normal

exercises.name: Standard Push-Up
exercises.simplyPut: Place your hands on the floor shoulder-width apart, lower your chest to the ground, then push yourself back up. It's like pressing the floor away from you. This is the most fundamental upper body exercise — soldiers, athletes, and grandparents all do push-ups.
exercises.howTo:
1. Place hands slightly wider than shoulder-width on the floor
2. Extend legs behind you, forming a straight line from head to heels
3. Brace your core tight — imagine someone about to poke your stomach
4. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the floor
5. Keep elbows at 45° angle (not flared out to sides)
6. Push through your palms to return to start
7. Exhale as you push up, inhale as you lower
8. Keep your neck neutral — look at a spot about 6 inches ahead of your fingers

exercises.contraindications: Shoulder impingement syndrome, acute wrist injury (carpal tunnel, fracture), rotator cuff tear, recent chest/sternum surgery. Modify to wall push-ups or incline push-ups for beginners or those with limitations.

exercises.name: Walking Dumbbell Lunge
exercises.simplyPut: Build strength in your legs with this intermediate-level exercise. Walking Dumbbell Lunge targets the legs while also engaging supporting muscle groups for balanced development.
exercises.howTo: Perform Walking Dumbbell Lunge with proper form focusing on legs activation.

mistakes.error: Ego lifting — using too much weight with poor form
mistakes.consequence: Disc herniation, muscle tears, joint damage
mistakes.correction: Reduce weight 20-30%; master form before adding load; video yourself

mistakes.consequence: Cold tissue tears, reduced performance
mistakes.correction: 2-3 progressive warm-up sets: empty bar → 50% → 70% → working weight

mistakes.consequence: Lumbar disc herniation (L4-L5, L5-S1 most common)
mistakes.correction: Hip hinge drill; brace before lifting; if you can't maintain neutral spine, weight is too heavy

mistakes.consequence: Reduced muscle activation; tendon overload at bottom position
mistakes.correction: 2-second pause at bottom of each rep; control the eccentric

mistakes.consequence: Muscle imbalances leading to compensatory injury over time
mistakes.correction: Include single-arm/single-leg work 1-2x per week

mistakes.consequence: Acute injury becomes chronic; compensation patterns develop
mistakes.correction: Pain ≠ effort. Sharp, shooting, or joint pain = stop. Muscle burn during set = normal
